content:
Hello all,

After submitting a patch for https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/1064, I'm ready to pick up something else.

I considered picking up another "good first issue" - but I understand those are reserved for people making their first code commits (something I certainly benefited from).

Could someone point me to another issue that might be a good "next first issue" for me to work on? I'm looking to deepen my understanding of the Rust portion of the kernel.
One thing that would be great to have is more examples in our documentation. That would also be something where you get to know the API better, since you have to infer what it does.
I will take a look at that! Ty!

Benno Lossin said:

One thing that would be great to have is more examples in our documentation.

What is the preferred way to go here?

Is it about expanding the in-code examples (=> KUnit tests are documentation tests)

/// # Examples
///
/// ```
/// put the example here;
/// ```

?

Or do we talk about putting additional examples into samples/rust?

Or? :wink:
I think the in-code examples.
I've now been pulled into some urgent things at work - will work on this as able.
I'm finally able to return to this this week!
Would it be possible to point me to a few files/functions/etc. that could use more documentation? I have been searching though the code base, but could use some pointers to where to start.
found one :)

@Nell Shamrell-Harrington There are some types that would be nice to add examples to, e.g. Opaque, ARef, Either.

Also the entire kernel::list could use a lot of examples, similar to what we have now in kernel::rbtree (which is great).

Thanks for taking a look!
For Either, @Wedson Almeida Filho's example on the FS get_or_crate_inode proposal would be a nice "advanced" one.
Thank you!
You're welcome!
Does the code for Rust binder live within the kernel source tree? Or does it live somewhere else? (it would be helpful to have it as a reference when I'm creating examples for structs that it uses)
It's not upstream, but you can find one version at https://android.googlesource.com/kernel/common/+/refs/heads/android-mainline-rust in drivers/android
aha! ty!

Miguel Ojeda said:

Nell Shamrell-Harrington There are some types that would be nice to add examples to, e.g. Opaque, ARef, Either.

Could anybody share two or three sentences whats the basic idea of Opaque? Or share a link? The best I was able to find is Opaque but that is not that verbose :wink:

Opaque<T> is used to wrap structs from the C side, so Opaque<bindings::mutex>. It gets rid of all the usual assumptions that Rust has for a value:

  • the value is allowed to be uninitialized (for example have invalid bit patters: 3 for a bool)
  • the value is allowed to be mutated, when a &Opaque<T> exists on the rust side
  • no uniqueness for mutable references: it is fine to have multiple &mut Opaque<T> point to the same value
  • the value is not allowed to be shared with other threads (ie it is !Sync)

We have to do this for all values that the C side has access to, because we can't ensure that the C side is adhering to the usual constraints that Rust needs.
Using Opaque allows us to continue to use references on the Rust side even for values shared with C.
this makes me wonder, should we also make it !Send? cc @team
I guess that is a matter of ergonomics. Maybe take a look at our use of Opaque and see I which cases we want the container Send or not?
In practice it wraps things containing raw pointers, so I don't think it makes much difference ergonomically.
You mean it would already be !Send and so it does not matter anyway?
oh yeah that's probably true.
It's by default !Send due to what Alice said, but you can impl Send for a struct that contains an Opaque if it's safe.
Patch for adding examples for Either is in :) https://lkml.org/lkml/2024/9/16/1086

Got some helpful feedback from the mailing list on my patch. One comment was that the rdocs usually have a more verbose explanation for the thing they are documenting.

I'm not sure how to add a more verbose explanation for Either, the current one seems pretty comprehensive. Any thoughts on this?

First, many thanks for working on this! And sorry for being unclear!

Nell Shamrell-Harrington said:

I'm not sure how to add a more verbose explanation for Either, the current one seems pretty comprehensive. Any thoughts on this?

What I wanted to say is that the existing Examples section in types.rs uses the style

# Examples

In the example below <following a one sentence description what the example does>

the example code

So what I wanted to propose is to add the # Examples tag and the one sentence In the example below ... "verbose" explanation to keep the example sections consistent in that file. Of course, in many other examples, this In the example below ... "verbose" explanation isn't there. So if there isn't anything reasonable to say thats fine, as well :grinning:
No worries at all! I appreciate the clarification!
Many thanks for sending version v2 of the patch!
